operator: CEA TGCC (Tres Grand Centre de Calcul du CEA)
login_host: Configured by site-specific TGCC access documentation; use an SSH alias such as 'irene'
scheduler: Slurm through TGCC Bridge commands (ccc_msub, ccc_mprun, ccc_mpp, ccc_mstat, ccc_mdel)
architecture: CPU-first heterogeneous x86_64 system: AMD Rome CPU partition plus specialized Intel/NVIDIA GPU and large-memory partitions

xlarge.name: Large-memory nodes
xlarge.gpu: 1 x NVIDIA Pascal P100 per node
xlarge.local_io: 2 x 1 TB HDD + 1.6 TB NVMe SSD per node

V100.name: GPU nodes
V100.gpu: 4 x NVIDIA Tesla V100 per node

V100l.name: GPU nodes with larger memory per GPU
V100l.gpu: 1 x NVIDIA Tesla V100 per node

V100xl.name: Large-memory GPU nodes
V100xl.gpu: 1 x NVIDIA Tesla V100 per node

notes: Irene is CPU-first in practice: the Rome partition has 2,286 regular CPU nodes and is the default target for normal MPI/CPU work.
Bridge is the user-facing batch interface. Submit scripts use #MSUB directives and ccc_msub; parallel work is launched with ccc_mprun.
The -q partition option and -A project option are mandatory for job submission.
On Irene, job submissions must declare filesystems with -m; without it, compute nodes cannot use scratch, work, or store.
The default job wall time is 7200 seconds if -T is omitted, but accurate wall time improves backfill scheduling.
For GPU partitions, GPUs are allocated indirectly by reserving enough CPU cores per task: roughly CpN/GpN cores per GPU.
Do not run heavy work on login nodes; use ccc_mprun -K or -s for interactive allocations, or submit a batch job.
Avoid high-frequency scheduler polling; the TGCC docs recommend an aggregate rate of only 1-2 scheduler queries per minute and prohibit watch on scheduler commands.

partitions.name: v100l-os
partitions.role: GPU OpenStack/OS partition as exposed by ccc_mpinfo

qos.normal: Default QoS, 1 day maximum in the example ccc_mqinfo output
qos.test: Debug/development QoS, faster scheduling, typically 30 minutes and limited concurrent jobs
qos.long: Longer jobs, up to 3 days in the example ccc_mqinfo output

modules.ccc: Mandatory site environment module loaded by default
modules.dfldatadir/own: Defines default CCCSCRATCHDIR, CCCWORKDIR, CCCSTOREDIR and related variables
modules.mpi/wi4mpi: MPI ABI wrapper for running with multiple MPI implementations
modules.nvhpc: NVIDIA HPC SDK for CUDA, OpenACC, OpenMP offload, and GPU libraries
modules.fftw3/mkl: FFTW interface backed by Intel MKL
modules.glost: Greedy Launcher of Small Tasks for embarrassingly parallel workloads

compilers.intel: Intel C/C++/Fortran compilers, MKL, and related toolchain modules
compilers.nvhpc: nvc, nvc++, nvfortran, nvcc for NVIDIA GPU programming
compilers.mpi_wrappers: mpicc, mpic++, mpif77, mpif90; default MPI implementation is loaded at connection time

storage.home: $HOME / $OWN_HOME / $OWN_CCFRHOME — NFS, backed up, 5 GB/user, intended for sources and scripts
storage.scratch: $CCCSCRATCHDIR / $CCFRSCRATCHDIR — Lustre, 300 GB/s, default 100 TB and 2M inodes per group, purges files not accessed for 60 days
storage.work: $CCCWORKDIR / $CCFRWORK — Lustre via routers, high bandwidth, not purged and not backed up, commonly 5 TB and 500k files per group
storage.store: $CCCSTOREDIR / $CCFRSTORE — Lustre + HSM, for large archived files; use ccc_hsm and ccc_pack/ccc_unpack
storage.tmp: $CCCTMPDIR / $CCFRTMP — local zram temporary space, purged after each job
storage.shm: $CCCSHMDIR — tmpfs, 50% of RAM, available during ccc_msub allocations and strongly recommended for small temporary files

containers: pcocc supports OCI images and limited SIF images. Import with pcocc-rs image import, run locally with pcocc-rs run, and run in batch with ccc_mprun -C <image> -- <cmd>.
support: TGCC Hotline; contact details are in internal project documentation.
